home.subtitle: An authentic experience aboard vintage carriages. Travel through National Parks and the most beautiful villages of the Central Apennines.
home.sponsorsAria: Partners and sponsors of Transiberiana d'Abruzzo
home.heroImageAlt: Historic train crossing a viaduct among the green mountains of Abruzzo

hero.subtitle: Designed in the late nineteenth century, the Sulmona-Isernia railway is a masterpiece of civil engineering that connected mountain communities before becoming Italy's most iconic tourist rail line.

timeline.title: Work begins
timeline.desc: Construction starts on the Sulmona-Isernia line. The engineering challenge is enormous: the route must overcome steep gradients by carving tunnels through the solid rock of the Apennines.

timeline.title: The inauguration
timeline.desc: The section as far as Cansano and Campo di Giove is inaugurated. The railway immediately becomes the main transport link for goods and passengers between Abruzzo and Molise.

timeline.title: Destruction and rebirth
timeline.desc: During the Second World War the line suffers severe damage because of the Gustav Line. In the 1950s it is rebuilt and modernized, returning to its role as a vital connector.

timeline.title: The suspension
timeline.desc: Regular passenger service is suspended because of high operating costs. People fear the definitive abandonment of the historic line.

timeline.title: The Transiberiana
timeline.desc: Reborn as a tourist railway thanks to Fondazione FS Italiane, it now offers the magic of travelling on vintage trains through the untouched landscapes of the Apennines.

stations.name: Sulmona
stations.desc: The city of sugared almonds and Ovid. Starting point of the historic itinerary.

stations.name: Campo di Giove
stations.desc: At the foot of the Majella, framed by dense woods and breathtaking landscapes.

stations.name: Palena
stations.desc: An isolated station in Quarto Santa Chiara, a realm of untamed nature.

stations.name: Roccaraso
stations.desc: The highest station on the line, famous for mountain tourism in both winter and summer.

stations.name: Castel di Sangro
stations.desc: Town of water and fly fishing, a key hub of the upper Sangro Valley.

stops.title: Stations along the journey
stops.subtitle: After the history, the route continues through villages and plateaus: every stop is a moment to enjoy slowly.

cta.title: Buy your ticket
cta.body: Leave your email and we will let you know as soon as bookings open for the next departures.

form.label: Email
form.success: Thanks! We will let you know as soon as bookings open.

storia.subtitle: Each stop opens as a visual, narrative and immersive chapter of the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo. From Sulmona to Isernia, the route becomes a sequence of towns, landscapes, traditions and local flavors.
storia.panelBody: The redesign replaces the classic descriptive window with a full experience: editorial hero, chapters, visual cues and a narrative rhythm across the towns of the route.

cards.label: Chapter 01
cards.title: Sulmona
cards.summary: A medieval town in the Peligna Valley, famous for its artisanal sugared almonds and for the medieval aqueduct with 21 arches crossing the historic center.
cards.story: Sulmona has very ancient origins. In pre-Roman times the area was inhabited by the Italic people of the Peligni and with the Roman conquest it became a municipium. Today it preserves a historic center rich in monuments, popular traditions, gastronomy and artistic heritage.

experience.intro: A town in the Peligna Valley with pre-Roman and Roman origins, famous for artisanal sugared almonds and for the medieval aqueduct with 21 arches.
experience.body: The town preserves historic churches and palaces and each year hosts cultural and traditional events celebrating its history and local craftsmanship.
experience.quote: Sulmona brings together ancient history, popular traditions, gastronomy and artistic heritage.

stats.label: Traditions
stats.value: Giostra Cavalleresca and Madonna che Scappa in Piazza

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: In pre-Roman times the area was inhabited by the Italic people of the Peligni. With the Roman conquest, the city became a municipium and developed thanks to its strategic position between the Adriatic and the inner Apennines.
chapters.pullout: Publius Ovidius Naso, one of the most important authors of Roman literature, was born here.

chapters.title: Patron saint and monuments
chapters.body: The symbol of the city is Sulmona's medieval aqueduct, built in the thirteenth century and formed by a long series of arches crossing Piazza Garibaldi, the main square. Among the most important religious buildings is San Panfilo Cathedral, dedicated to the patron saint of the city, together with numerous historic churches and old palaces.
chapters.pullout: The historic center preserves monuments and artistic evidence among the most representative of the city.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Among the most important traditions is the Giostra Cavalleresca of Sulmona, a historical reenactment held every summer in which the riders of the city's districts compete in a tournament inspired by the Middle Ages. Another very famous celebration is the Madonna che Scappa in Piazza, held on Easter morning.
chapters.pullout: Each year the city hosts cultural and traditional events celebrating its history and local craftsmanship.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Sulmona is famous throughout Italy for the production of sugared almonds, sweets made of almonds covered in sugar, produced by hand and often arranged in decorative flower compositions. Local cuisine also includes dishes based on legumes such as lentils and chickpeas, maccheroni alla chitarra, lamb and sheep meat, and traditional desserts such as torcinelli and calcionetti abruzzesi.
chapters.pullout: These flavors reflect a rural tradition that combines simplicity, local ingredients and ancient preparation techniques.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Thanks to its position in the Peligna Valley and its proximity to the mountains and natural parks of the Apennines, Sulmona is today also an important starting point for excursions and nature tourism.
chapters.pullout: Its location between the Peligna Valley and the Apennines makes Sulmona a natural stop between the city, the mountains and routes through the landscape.

gallery.alt: View of Sulmona with a historic urban atmosphere
gallery.caption: Medieval aqueduct and Piazza Garibaldi.

gallery.alt: Placeholder detail for Sulmona architecture or craftsmanship
gallery.caption: Artisanal sugared almonds and decorative flower compositions.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for a public square or ritual scene in Sulmona
gallery.caption: City traditions and public celebrations.

rituals.title: Giostra Cavalleresca
rituals.desc: Summer historical reenactment in which the riders of the city's districts compete in a tournament inspired by the Middle Ages.

rituals.title: Madonna che Scappa in Piazza
rituals.desc: Easter celebration in which the statue of the Madonna runs toward the statue of the risen Christ among the crowd gathered in the square.

rituals.title: Artisanal sugared almonds
rituals.desc: Ancient artisanal tradition linked to sugar-coated almonds and decorative compositions shaped like flowers.

closing.title: History, traditions, gastronomy and landscape
closing.body: Sulmona brings together ancient history, popular traditions, gastronomy and artistic heritage, making it one of the most interesting cultural centers in Abruzzo.

cards.label: Chapter 02
cards.title: Campo di Giove
cards.summary: Mountain village in the Majella Park, around 1100 meters above sea level, among beech woods, trails and wide-open Apennine views.
cards.story: Campo di Giove has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to transhumance and commercial routes. Today it preserves stone architecture, local celebrations and a strong bond with the landscape of the Majella.

experience.intro: A mountain village in the Majella Park, around 1100 meters above sea level, among beech woods, trails and Apennine landscapes.
experience.body: Campo di Giove has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center tied to transhumance. Today it preserves paved streets, stone houses, small squares and a strong natural vocation.
experience.quote: Campo di Giove brings together mountain memory, local traditions and the landscapes of the Majella.

stats.label: Monuments
stats.value: San Vincenzo church and local stone

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Campo di Giove has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center along routes linked to trade and transhumance. Its history is closely tied to pastoral life and to the landscape of the Majella.
chapters.pullout: The village grew along routes of exchange and pastoral movement between mountain slopes and valleys.

chapters.title: Historic center and monuments
chapters.body: The village preserves paved streets, stone houses and small squares that retain the character of a mountain settlement. Among the most important religious buildings is San Vincenzo church, alongside smaller chapels scattered through the historic fabric.
chapters.pullout: The urban fabric preserves the compact scale of the mountain centers of the Majella.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Village life is marked by patron feasts, processions and local fairs that keep community gatherings alive. These celebrations accompany the civil and religious calendar of the village.
chapters.pullout: The local calendar brings together devotion, public gathering and the memory of the village.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes sheep and goat cheeses, cured meats, honey and mountain sweets. Among the best-known dishes are homemade maccheroni, gnocchi, legume soups, roasted lamb and goat and bread baked in a wood-fired oven.
chapters.pullout: Mountain cooking comes from livestock, simple products and domestic preparation.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Thanks to its position in the Majella Park, Campo di Giove is today a point of reference for trekking, walks and nature itineraries through woods and mountains.
chapters.pullout: The Majella, the beech woods and the trails make the village a natural gateway to hiking.

gallery.alt: Mountain view of Campo di Giove in the Majella Park
gallery.caption: Mountain landscape and beech woods of the Majella Park.

gallery.alt: Detail of the historic fabric of Campo di Giove
gallery.caption: Paved streets, stone houses and village squares.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for traditions or cuisine in Campo di Giove
gallery.caption: Local traditions and mountain cooking.

rituals.title: Patron feasts
rituals.desc: The religious celebrations gather the community at the main moments of the year.

rituals.title: Processions
rituals.desc: Processions accompany celebrations and keep the religious dimension of the village alive.

rituals.title: Local fairs
rituals.desc: Public gatherings connect tradition, encounter and mountain village life.

closing.title: Majella, stone and mountain life
closing.body: Campo di Giove preserves the memory of transhumance, stone architecture, local traditions and a strong vocation for the landscape of the Majella.

cards.label: Chapter 03
cards.title: Palena
cards.summary: Mountain village near the Forchetta pass, between the Majella, the high plateaus and one of the route's most panoramic railway points.
cards.story: Palena has ancient and medieval origins and developed as a pastoral and agricultural center. Today it maintains a strong bond with San Falco, with its railway station and with the upland landscape around the village.

experience.intro: A mountain village tied to the Majella and the high plateaus, near the Forchetta pass and one of the most panoramic railway points on the route.
experience.body: Palena has ancient and medieval origins and developed as a pastoral and agricultural center. Today it maintains a close link with San Falco, with the railway station and with the high-altitude landscape.
experience.quote: Palena brings together devotion, mountain landscape and railway memory.

stats.label: Monuments
stats.value: San Falco church and the station

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Palena has ancient and medieval origins and developed as a mountain center tied to pastoral life and agriculture. Its history is linked to pastures, trails and upland rural activity.
chapters.pullout: The village grew within a mountain economy shaped by livestock and agricultural work.

chapters.title: Patron saint and monuments
chapters.body: Among the most important places is San Falco church, linked to the patron saint of the town. Another identity landmark is the railway station on the high plateaus, connected to one of the most panoramic stretches of the journey.
chapters.pullout: San Falco and the station tell the religious and railway identity of Palena.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Village life is still marked by patron feasts, processions and historical reenactments that keep alive the relationship between community, religious memory and the story of the town.
chapters.pullout: Public celebrations renew the link between the community and its history.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local flavors include homemade maccheroni, legume soups, roasts, cheeses, honey and chestnuts. Traditional sweets include mountain zeppole, almond and walnut sweets and bread baked in a wood-fired oven.
chapters.pullout: Palena's table reflects the mountain landscape and its simplest and most enduring products.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Its proximity to the Majella, to the plateaus and to mountain trails makes Palena a starting point for excursions, walks and upland nature routes.
chapters.pullout: Majella, plateaus and trails make Palena a stop of landscape and mountain breathing space.

gallery.alt: View of Palena between mountains and plateaus
gallery.caption: Palena between the Majella, the plateaus and the railway landscape.

gallery.alt: Placeholder detail for church or station in Palena
gallery.caption: San Falco church and the memory of the station on the high plateaus.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for traditions or cuisine in Palena
gallery.caption: Local traditions and the flavors of the mountain village.

rituals.title: Patron feasts
rituals.desc: Patron celebrations mark the most felt moments of village life.

rituals.title: Processions
rituals.desc: Processions keep alive the religious and public dimension of the community.

rituals.title: Historical reenactments
rituals.desc: Historical events bring episodes and symbols of local history back into the present.

closing.title: Palena between devotion and landscape
closing.body: Palena brings together mountain roots, local faith, railway memory and a cuisine tied to upland life.

cards.label: Chapter 04
cards.title: Roccaraso
cards.summary: A mountain town in Alto Sangro, between winter sports, summer trails and high-altitude open landscapes.
cards.story: Roccaraso has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to commerce and local activity. Today it is one of the main tourist destinations of the Apennines, visited in winter and in summer.

experience.intro: A mountain town in Alto Sangro, known for winter tourism and for outdoor activities that animate the town in summer too.
experience.body: Roccaraso has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to commerce. Today it combines religious and civil architecture, village traditions and a strong tourist vocation.
experience.quote: Roccaraso brings together mountain architecture, village traditions and upland tourism.

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Roccaraso has medieval origins and developed as a mountain center linked to local trade and the activities of the territory. Its growth is connected to upland life and to the routes of Alto Sangro.
chapters.pullout: The history of the town begins from the encounter between mountain settlement, exchange and high-altitude life.

chapters.title: Monuments
chapters.body: Among the most important buildings is Santa Maria Assunta church, together with noble palaces that testify to the urban history of the town. These places define the historic face of Roccaraso.
chapters.pullout: Church and palaces tell the passage between religious, civil and architectural memory.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Religious feasts and village festivals keep Roccaraso's community calendar alive. In these moments, typical local products also take center stage, linked to local culture and conviviality.
chapters.pullout: The town's celebrations bring together the religious dimension, public gathering and products of the territory.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes homemade pasta, soups, roasts and stews, cheeses and honey. Among the sweets are zeppole, dried-fruit tarts and bread baked in a wood-fired oven, in a table shaped by the mountains and by seasonality.
chapters.pullout: Roccaraso's cuisine comes from simple ingredients, slow time and mountain flavors.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Roccaraso occupies a central position in the landscapes of Alto Sangro and today is one of the main bases for winter sports, summer trekking and mountain-bike routes.
chapters.pullout: The geography of Alto Sangro makes Roccaraso a natural stop for the mountains in every season.

gallery.alt: View of Roccaraso between architecture and mountain landscape
gallery.caption: Roccaraso between mountain town and the landscapes of Alto Sangro.

gallery.alt: Placeholder detail for architecture or monuments of Roccaraso
gallery.caption: Santa Maria Assunta church and the architectural traces of the town.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for sports or seasonal life in Roccaraso
gallery.caption: Mountain tourism between winter and summer.

rituals.title: Religious feasts
rituals.desc: Religious celebrations keep the collective dimension of the town alive.

rituals.title: Village festivals
rituals.desc: Festivals bring together public gathering, local cuisine and mountain identity.

rituals.title: Typical products
rituals.desc: Products of the territory accompany festivities and moments of local conviviality.

closing.title: Roccaraso between architecture and tourism
closing.body: Roccaraso weaves together mountain history, local architecture, village traditions and activities tied to the seasons of Alto Sangro.

cards.label: Chapter 05
cards.title: Castel di Sangro
cards.summary: A center of Alto Sangro between fortified origins, stone streets, places of art and a strong cultural vocation.
cards.story: Castel di Sangro was born as a fortified medieval settlement around the castle and developed as the center of the valley. Today it preserves lanes, squares, religious monuments and a strong cultural life.

experience.intro: A center of Alto Sangro with fortified origins, stone streets, religious monuments and one of the main cultural poles of the valley.
experience.body: Castel di Sangro was born as a medieval nucleus around the castle and developed as the center of the valley. Today it combines lanes, squares, sacred art, a museum and cultural activity.
experience.quote: Castel di Sangro interweaves medieval memory, cultural life and the cuisine of Alto Sangro.

stats.label: Monuments
stats.value: Santa Maria Assunta and the gallery

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Castel di Sangro was born as a fortified medieval settlement developed around the castle. From this nucleus the center of the valley took shape, becoming a place of passage and urban life in Alto Sangro.
chapters.pullout: The first layout of the town is linked to defensive functions and control of the territory.

chapters.title: Monuments and historic center
chapters.body: The historic center preserves lanes, squares and stone architecture that define the face of the town. Among the most important places are Santa Maria Assunta church and the Patiniana Gallery, a cultural reference for the city.
chapters.pullout: Among stone lanes and places of art, the center tells the urban role of Castel di Sangro.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: The life of the town is accompanied by patron feasts, historical reenactments and local fairs that renew collective memory and the link with the territory.
chapters.pullout: Public celebrations keep civic identity, local history and community participation together.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes maccheroni alla chitarra with lamb or wild boar sauce, tacconelli, pecorini, guanciale and other cured meats. Among the sweets are ricotta and chestnut zeppole, together with local bread and honey.
chapters.pullout: The table of Castel di Sangro combines traditional pasta, meats, cheeses and sweets of the valley.

chapters.title: Location
chapters.body: Its position in the upper Sangro Valley and its proximity to the mountains make Castel di Sangro a reference center for tourism and for the cultural life of the valley.
chapters.pullout: Between valley, mountain and town, Castel di Sangro keeps a hinge role in the territory.

gallery.alt: View of Castel di Sangro between architecture and valley
gallery.caption: Stone-built historic center and the landscape of Alto Sangro.

gallery.alt: Placeholder detail for monuments or collections in Castel di Sangro
gallery.caption: Santa Maria Assunta and the Patiniana Gallery.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for traditions or cuisine in Castel di Sangro
gallery.caption: Local traditions and the cuisine of the valley.

rituals.title: Patron feasts
rituals.desc: Patron celebrations continue to mark the public rhythm of the town.

rituals.title: Historical reenactments
rituals.desc: Reenactment events bring episodes and atmospheres of local history back to the center.

rituals.title: Local fairs
rituals.desc: Fairs keep alive the relationship between town, territory and local production.

closing.title: Castel di Sangro between culture and valley
closing.body: Castel di Sangro preserves medieval origins, places of art, urban traditions and a cuisine that reflects the landscape of Alto Sangro.

cards.label: Chapter 06
cards.title: Isernia
cards.summary: An ancient city of Molise, between the Paleolithic site of Isernia La Pineta, historic monuments and a strong urban identity.
cards.story: Isernia sinks its roots into the Paleolithic and crosses the Samnite, Roman and medieval eras. Today it is a cultural and administrative center that brings together monuments, traditions, craftsmanship and gastronomy.

experience.intro: An ancient city of Molise, known for the Paleolithic site of Isernia La Pineta and for its historic center with churches, palaces, museums and monumental fountains.
experience.body: Isernia crosses the Samnite, Roman and medieval eras and today keeps a strategic role from a cultural and administrative point of view, together with a strong artisanal and gastronomic identity.
experience.quote: Isernia brings together millenary roots, monuments, traditions and the flavors of inland Molise.

stats.label: Traditions
stats.value: Holy Week and the feast of Saint Peter

stats.label: Flavors
stats.value: Cavatelli, cured meats and honey sweets

chapters.title: Origins
chapters.body: Isernia sinks its roots into the Paleolithic, as shown by the site of Isernia La Pineta. This earliest phase is followed by the Samnite and Roman eras, while in the Middle Ages the city developed as a religious and commercial center.
chapters.pullout: From prehistory to the Middle Ages, Isernia preserves one of the broadest historical stratifications of the route.

chapters.title: Monuments and historic center
chapters.body: Among the most important places are San Pietro Apostolo Cathedral, San Francesco church, Palazzo San Francesco, Fontana Fraterna and Piazza Andrea d'Isernia. These spaces define the urban identity of the city.
chapters.pullout: Cathedral, fountain, palaces and squares compose the historic profile of the city center.

chapters.title: Traditions
chapters.body: Among the most deeply felt traditions are the Holy Week processions, the feast of Saint Peter and summer food fairs. Alongside them are iron craftsmanship, textiles and markets that keep the urban character of the city alive.
chapters.pullout: The traditions of Isernia connect devotion, markets and artisanal knowledge.

chapters.title: Flavors
chapters.body: Local cuisine includes cavatelli with turnip greens or sausage sauce, potato-and-ricotta gnocchi, tagliatelle with lamb or goat ragout, Molise pecorino, soppressata and capocollo. Among the sweets are ricotta fritters, zeppole, almond-and-honey biscuits, homemade bread and mountain honey.
chapters.pullout: The table of Isernia brings together traditional pasta, cured meats, dairy products and sweets of inland Molise.

chapters.title: Current role
chapters.body: Today Isernia is a strategic city from the cultural and administrative point of view. Its contemporary identity brings together millenary history, medieval and baroque architecture, living traditions and gastronomy.
chapters.pullout: In the present, Isernia is recognized as a city of history, urban functions and widespread memory.

gallery.caption: Isernia between urban history, monuments and the memory of the route.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for monuments or fountains of Isernia
gallery.caption: Fontana Fraterna, the cathedral and the historic center.

gallery.alt: Placeholder for traditions or cuisine in Isernia
gallery.caption: Traditions, craftsmanship and the flavors of the city.

rituals.title: Holy Week
rituals.desc: The Holy Week processions are among the most deeply felt moments of the city's calendar.

rituals.title: Feast of Saint Peter
rituals.desc: The feast dedicated to Saint Peter accompanies one of the most recognizable religious events of the city.

rituals.title: Markets and craftsmanship
rituals.desc: Ironwork, textiles and markets keep the artisanal and commercial tradition of Isernia alive.

closing.title: Isernia between prehistory and historic city
closing.body: Isernia brings together the Paleolithic site, monuments of the center, city traditions and a cuisine that tells the story of inland Molise.

overlay.ritualsTitle: What the city puts back on stage
overlay.finalNote: The first chapter is a threshold: from here history gains rhythm, material and landscape.
overlay.placeholderNote: For now this city opens in a compact format, with a narrative summary designed to accompany the journey.

stops.subtitle: Explore the historic stations along Italy's highest and most scenic railway, a ribbon of steel stitching together national parks and nature reserves.

activities.title: What to do during the stops
activities.subtitle: Choose the season and discover what to do in the towns where the train stops, with suggestions tailored to the length of each stop.
activities.note: Indicative durations: we will update the timetable details as soon as they are available.

seasonCopy.spring: Blossoms and fresh air: gentle walks and slower rhythms.
seasonCopy.summer: Long days: look for shade, fresh water and wide-open views.
seasonCopy.autumn: Warm colors and seasonal flavors: perfect for an easy pace.
seasonCopy.winter: Mountain atmosphere: short activities and warm indoor breaks.

rules.title: On-board rules
rules.body: Historic trains require care. Do not lean out of the windows while the train is moving, do not throw rubbish from the train, and behave respectfully toward the vintage materials and other passengers.

faq.q: Is the train heated in winter?
faq.a: Yes, all the vintage carriages are equipped with original restored and working steam heating and radiators.

faq.q: Can I bring my dog on board?
faq.a: Small dogs are allowed free of charge inside the appropriate carrier. Larger dogs require a dedicated ticket.
Yes, although they are the original 1930s ones. For greater comfort we suggest using the toilets available at the stations during longer stops.

faq.q: Can I bring food on board?
faq.a: Absolutely. There is no restaurant car, so bringing your own packed lunch is welcome, especially during longer stops in the villages.

meteo.title: Live weather
meteo.subtitle: Real-time weather conditions along the Transiberiana d'Abruzzo.

empty.title: No data available
empty.dark: The station sensors are sleeping deeply. The weather owl is keeping watch. Try again in a few minutes.
empty.light: The station sensors are sleeping. Try again in a few minutes, the weather bunny is on the job.

error.title: Connection lost
error.dark: The candle has gone out, we cannot reach the sensors. Check the connection and try again.
error.light: We cannot reach the IoT sensors. Check the connection and try again.

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.body: The page you are looking for does not exist or has been moved. You can go back to the home page and continue the journey.
